topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating persistence, mirroring the low thrumming in Elias’s chest. He stared at the framed commendations on his desk, the polished metal catching the harsh light, each achievement feeling suddenly hollow. Sarah, across the cubicle partition, was laughing—a bright, unrestrained sound that grated on his nerves. He meticulously sharpened his pencil, the small, precise movements a desperate attempt to impose order on the internal chaos. Every time the manager’s office door swung open, Elias felt a tightening in his jaw, a stubborn refusal to meet anyone’s gaze, as if looking directly at the decision meant inviting some kind of inevitable disappointment. He picked at a loose thread on his sleeve, his eyes fixed on a distant point on the wall, tracing patterns that led nowhere.

[story 2]

"Another round of coffee, Elias?" Sarah asked, leaning over his desk, her voice warm and entirely too cheerful. He managed a tight nod, the movement stiff. He didn't look up. The air in the shared workspace felt thick, suffocating. He could feel the heat rising in his neck, a flush that had nothing to do with the temperature of the room. He focused intensely on the condensation forming on his water glass, watching the droplets slowly distort the office landscape. When the topic of the upcoming review came up, his response was clipped, low, almost a monotone. He felt a profound weight settle on his shoulders, the kind that makes every breath feel like an effort, as he silently cataloged the subtle, almost imperceptible advantages Sarah possessed, a tally he refused to acknowledge aloud.

[story 3]

Mark paced the length of his small office, the worn carpet muffling the sound of his restless steps. He stopped by the window, the city sprawl below a dizzying tapestry of indifferent lights. His knuckles were white where he gripped the windowsill. The silence in the room was heavy, amplifying the frantic rhythm of his own heartbeat. He replayed the meeting in his head, every word dissected, searching for the flaw, the moment where he had faltered. He felt a deep, internal churning, a sensation like sediment shifting in still water. When Sarah approached him later, offering a tentative smile, Mark simply looked down at his hands, turning them over slowly, as if examining them for some hidden defect. His reply was measured, careful, laced with an almost painful restraint, every syllable chosen with deliberate, cold precision.
